topics.title: How JavaScript Executes Code
importance: Before learning frameworks or architecture, understand how JavaScript reads, prepares, and executes code. This knowledge helps debug scope problems, asynchronous behavior, memory issues, and unexpected runtime results.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: A JavaScript engine executes JavaScript code.
Examples include V8, SpiderMonkey, and JavaScriptCore.
The engine first parses the source code.
Parsing converts source code into an Abstract Syntax Tree.
The engine may generate bytecode before converting frequently used code into optimized machine code.
Runtime assumptions may cause optimized code to be deoptimized.
Frequently changing value types can make optimization harder.

groups.title: Important questions
items: What happens after a JavaScript file is downloaded?
What is the difference between parsing and execution?
Why can dynamically typed code be harder to optimize?
What causes JavaScript engines to deoptimize code?

groups.title: Practical mastery
items: Inspect JavaScript execution using browser developer tools.
Compare execution of stable-type and changing-type functions.
Learn to identify optimization-sensitive patterns without prematurely optimizing code.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Source code is broken into meaningful tokens.
Tokens are converted into a tree representing the code structure.
Syntax errors are generally detected during parsing.
Tools such as ESLint, Babel, TypeScript, and minifiers work with syntax trees.
An AST represents meaning and structure rather than the original formatting.

groups.title: Practical mastery
items: Inspect how arrow functions, classes, and optional chaining appear in an AST.
Understand how Babel transforms modern syntax into older JavaScript.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: JavaScript prepares an environment before executing code.
The global execution context is created first.
Every function call creates a new function execution context.
Variables, function declarations, and scope relationships are prepared before line-by-line execution.
Execution contexts are placed on the call stack.
An execution context disappears from the stack when the function finishes, but referenced lexical data may remain because of closures.

groups.title: Practical mastery
Key things to understand
items: Every function call adds a frame to the call stack.
The most recently called function executes first.
When a function finishes, its frame is removed.
Very deep or infinite recursion can exceed the stack limit.
The call stack is one reason normal JavaScript execution is described as single-threaded.

groups.title: Practical mastery

topics.title: JavaScript Variables, Scope, and Hoisting
importance: Scope determines where data can be accessed. Poor understanding of scope causes hidden bugs, accidental mutation, stale values, and naming conflicts.

groups.title: Important things about var
items: Does not respect normal block scope
Can create accidental bugs in loops and conditional blocks

groups.title: Important things about let
items: Cannot be redeclared in the same scope
Exists in the Temporal Dead Zone before declaration

groups.title: Important things about const
items: Does not make objects deeply immutable

groups.title: Key distinction
prose: The variable reference cannot be replaced, but the object can still be mutated.

groups.title: Global scope
items: Accessible across large parts of the program.
Global variables can conflict with other scripts.
Browser globals can become properties of window, depending on declaration style.

groups.title: Function scope
items: Variables declared inside a function are unavailable outside it.
Each function invocation receives its own local environment.

groups.title: Block scope
items: Created by blocks such as if, for, and {}.
let and const respect block scope.
var does not behave as block-scoped.

groups.title: Module scope
items: Variables declared inside ES modules are private to that module unless exported.
ES modules run in strict mode.

groups.title: Lexical scope
items: Scope is determined by where code is written.
It is not determined by where a function is called.
Inner functions can access variables from outer scopes.

groups.title: Key things to understand
prose: When JavaScript cannot find a variable locally, it looks upward through the lexical environment chain.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Hoisting does not physically move code.
JavaScript registers declarations while creating execution environments.
Function declarations are available before their written position.
var is registered and initialized with undefined.
let and const are registered but remain uninitialized.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: let and const exist before their declaration line.
They cannot be accessed until initialization.
The period before initialization is called the Temporal Dead Zone.

groups.title: Key things to understand
prose: A variable in an inner scope may hide a variable with the same name from an outer scope.
The local value shadows the global value.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Excessive shadowing reduces readability and can cause developers to use the wrong variable accidentally.

topics.title: JavaScript Data Types and Values
importance: Understanding values and references is necessary for equality, mutation, copying, React state updates, memoization, and performance.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Primitive comparison generally compares the actual value.
Variables contain primitive values directly from the language-level perspective.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Two separate objects with identical properties are not strictly equal.
Mutating an object affects every reference pointing to that object.

groups.title: Key things to understand
prose: This happens because many decimal fractions cannot be represented exactly in binary floating-point form.

groups.title: Important practices
items: Avoid raw floating-point arithmetic for financial calculations.
Store money in the smallest currency unit, such as paise or cents.
Use BigInt only where its limitations are acceptable.

groups.title: Important things
items: It is not equal to itself.
Number.isNaN() is safer than the global isNaN().

groups.title: undefined
items: A value has not been assigned.
A function did not return a value.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: JavaScript does not enforce a universal semantic difference.
Optional chaining and nullish coalescing help work with nullish values.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Objects and arrays are truthy, even when empty.
Implicit coercion may produce surprising results.

groups.title: Loose equality
prose: Performs coercion according to complex language rules.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Structural equality requires custom comparison logic.

topics.title: Functions and Functional Concepts
importance: Functions are central to JavaScript. React components, hooks, callbacks, middleware, event handlers, and asynchronous operations all depend on function behavior.

groups.title: Key differences
items: Cannot normally be used as constructors

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Parameters belong to the function declaration.
Arguments are values supplied during invocation.
Rest parameters create a real array.
Default values apply when the argument is undefined.
prose: Passing null does not activate the default.

groups.title: Important concepts
items: Accepts a function as an argument.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: When or how it should happen

groups.title: Important characteristics
items: Same input produces the same output.
Does not produce hidden side effects.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Not every function can or should be pure. Network requests, logging, storage access, and DOM updates are side effects.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Every recursive function needs a terminating base case.
Deep recursion may cause stack overflow.
Iterative solutions may be more memory-efficient.
Recursion is natural for nested data such as trees and menus.

groups.title: Partial application
prose: Creates a new function with some arguments already supplied.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Currying produces a chain of single-argument functions.
Partial application can bind one or more arguments.
These techniques are useful for reusable configuration and composition.
Excessive currying may reduce readability in ordinary application code.

groups.title: Debounce
prose: Executes after calls stop for a defined duration.

groups.title: Throttle
prose: Limits execution to at most once per interval.

topics.title: Closures
importance: Closures power private state, callbacks, React hooks, memoization, event handlers, and many reusable JavaScript patterns.

groups.title: Important concept
prose: A closure is created when a function remembers variables from the lexical environment where it was created.
The returned function retains access to count.

groups.title: Key things to understand
prose: Closures can protect data from direct external modification.

groups.title: Important concepts
prose: A function factory creates configured functions.

groups.title: Important things
prose: A stale closure occurs when a callback captures an older value that no longer represents the current state.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Closures capture variables from a particular render or execution environment.
React creates a new function scope on every render.
Older callbacks may continue referring to older render values.

groups.title: Key practice
items: Avoid retaining entire objects when only a small value is needed.
prose: Release references when they are no longer required.

topics.title: The this Keyword
importance: Understanding this helps with object methods, legacy React code, callbacks, classes, and library APIs.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: In a normal function call, this depends on execution mode.
In strict mode, this is undefined.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: When a function is called through an object, the object before the dot becomes the receiver.

groups.title: Overview
prose: The function is no longer called through user.

groups.title: call
prose: Calls immediately with individually supplied arguments.

groups.title: apply
prose: Calls immediately with arguments supplied as an array-like value.

groups.title: bind
prose: Returns a new function with a fixed this.

groups.title: Key thing
items: this refers to the new object.
The object is returned unless another object is explicitly returned.
prose: When a function is called with new:

groups.title: Key things
items: Arrow functions do not create their own this.
They capture this from the surrounding lexical scope.
They are not always suitable for object methods.

topics.title: Objects, Prototypes, and Classes
importance: JavaScript uses prototype-based inheritance. Understanding it is necessary for classes, object behavior, library internals, and efficient design.

groups.title: Overview
Important things
items: Methods placed on the prototype are shared.
Methods created inside the constructor are recreated for every instance.
new controls object creation and prototype linkage.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Classes use the prototype system internally.
Class methods are placed on the prototype.
Static methods belong to the class itself.

groups.title: Composition
prose: Builds behavior by combining smaller capabilities.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Subclasses override large portions of parent behavior.
Reuse does not represent a true identity relationship.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Whether access is handled through getters and setters.

topics.title: Arrays and Data Transformation
importance: Frontend applications constantly transform server responses, lists, tables, forms, and UI state.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: These methods modify the original array.
Mutation can cause bugs in React state and Redux reducers.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Choose the method that best communicates intention.
Avoid using reduce for everything merely because it is flexible.

groups.title: Purpose
prose: Transforms every item and returns a new array.

groups.title: Important points
items: Result length normally matches input length.
Do not use map purely for side effects.

groups.title: Purpose
prose: Keeps values that match a condition.

groups.title: Important points
items: Filtering large collections repeatedly may need memoization or indexing.

groups.title: Purpose
prose: Combines a list into another value.

groups.title: Important points
items: Always consider an explicit initial value.
Break complex reductions into named helper functions.

groups.title: Map
items: Keys are not limited to strings.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: React state containing Map or Set still requires immutable replacement to trigger predictable updates.

topics.title: Asynchronous JavaScript and Event Loop
importance: Frontend applications depend on timers, events, network requests, promises, rendering, and user interactions.

groups.title: Key things
items: Long-running synchronous code blocks the main thread.
While blocked, the browser cannot respond smoothly to input or render updates.

groups.title: Key things to understand
items: Browser APIs are not part of the JavaScript language itself.
They allow work to happen outside the current call stack.
Their callbacks are scheduled for later execution.

groups.title: Key things
items: A task executes only when the call stack is empty.
Timer duration represents a minimum delay, not exact execution time.

groups.title: Key things
items: Microtasks normally run before the next task.
The microtask queue is drained after the current stack finishes.
Too many microtasks can delay rendering and task processing.

groups.title: Key things
items: The browser decides it is the appropriate time.
prose: The browser may render between tasks when:

groups.title: Important caution
prose: A long task or endless microtask chain can delay paint.

groups.title: Important things
items: setTimeout does not guarantee exact timing.
Background tabs may have stronger timer throttling.

groups.title: Overview
Reason
items: Microtasks execute before the next task.

topics.title: Promises, Async/Await, and Request Control
importance: Reliable asynchronous architecture requires more than writing await. You need error handling, cancellation, concurrency control, deduplication, and race-condition management.

groups.title: Key things
items: A promise changes state only once.
A fulfilled or rejected promise is settled.
Promise handlers execute asynchronously through the microtask queue.

groups.title: Important things
items: Return values flow into the next .then.
Returned promises are awaited by the chain.

groups.title: Key things
items: Errors travel down the promise chain until handled.
A catch may recover by returning a normal value.
finally is for cleanup, not replacing the result.

groups.title: Important things
items: An async function always returns a promise.
Returning a value creates a fulfilled promise.

groups.title: Key things
items: await pauses only the current async function.
It does not block the entire browser thread.
Code after await continues as a microtask.
Independent requests should not be awaited sequentially without reason.

groups.title: Sequential
code:
const user = await getUser();
const orders = await getOrders();

groups.title: Parallel
Promise.all
items: One failure should reject the complete operation.

groups.title: Promise.race
items: The first settled result should win.

groups.title: Promise.any
items: The first successful result should win.
Rejections should be ignored until every option fails.

groups.title: Key things
items: Cancellation prevents unnecessary work and stale updates.
Pass the same signal through relevant layers.
Do not display alarming error messages for intentional aborts.

groups.title: Common example
prose: The first request may finish after the second and replace the newer result.

groups.title: Solutions
items: Use server-state libraries with cancellation and cache keys.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Retry only when failures may be temporary.

groups.title: Key things
prose: Do not start thousands of requests simultaneously. Use a queue or promise pool that limits active work.

topics.title: Browser Architecture
importance: Understanding browser internals helps explain rendering performance, memory behavior, security boundaries, workers, and UI responsiveness.

groups.title: Important things
items: A renderer process usually handles a page or group of related pages.
Process isolation improves stability and security.
Crashing one renderer should not necessarily crash the entire browser.

groups.title: Key things
items: Heavy JavaScript blocks rendering and input.
Long tasks create poor interaction responsiveness.
Work should be divided or moved away from the main thread where appropriate.

groups.title: Important things
items: Workers execute JavaScript on another thread.
Workers cannot directly access the DOM.
Data may be cloned or transferred.
Workers are suitable for CPU-heavy computations.

groups.title: Key things
items: Different origins may be separated into different renderer processes.
Sandboxing limits what renderer processes can access.
Browser security relies on several isolation layers.
Same-origin rules remain essential even with process isolation.

topics.title: Browser Rendering Pipeline
importance: Rendering knowledge helps optimize animations, layouts, large pages, responsive interfaces, and interaction performance.

groups.title: Important things
items: Scripts may pause parsing depending on how they are loaded.
Invalid HTML may be corrected by browser parsing rules.

groups.title: Important things
items: CSS rules are parsed into the CSS Object Model.
The browser needs styling information to render correctly.
Complex selectors may increase style calculation work, although real-world impact depends on scale.

groups.title: Important things
items: DOM and CSSOM contribute to the render tree.
Non-visible elements such as display: none are excluded.
The render tree represents elements that require visual output.

groups.title: Important things
items: Layers are combined into the final page.
transform and opacity can often be handled at the compositing stage.
Separate layers may improve animation smoothness.
Too many layers increase memory usage.

groups.title: Problem pattern
prose: This may force repeated synchronous layout calculations.

groups.title: Better approach
items: Avoid mixing measurement and mutation repeatedly.

topics.title: Networking Fundamentals
importance: Frontend engineers need to understand what happens between the browser, CDN, load balancer, API gateway, BFF, and backend services.

groups.title: Key things
items: Fragments are generally not sent to the server.
Query parameters are part of the request URL.
Sensitive information should not be placed in URLs.
URLs may be logged by browsers, servers, analytics, and proxies.

groups.title: Important things
items: DNS converts domain names into IP addresses.
Results may be cached at multiple levels.
DNS lookup adds latency when uncached.
CDNs may use DNS to route users to nearby infrastructure.

groups.title: Important things
items: TCP provides ordered, reliable data delivery.
Connection reuse reduces repeated setup cost.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: HTTPS does not make application logic secure by itself. XSS, CSRF, insecure authorization, and data exposure can still exist.

groups.title: Key things
items: POST usually creates or triggers processing.
PUT generally replaces a complete resource.
OPTIONS is often used for CORS preflight.

groups.title: Important definition
prose: An operation is idempotent when repeating it produces the same intended server state.

groups.title: Key strategy
prose: Use idempotency keys for operations that must not be duplicated.

groups.title: Key things
prose: Do not handle every failure with the same generic message.

groups.title: Key things
items: Authorization tokens and cookies serve different transport patterns.

groups.title: HTTP/3
items: Faster connection establishment in many situations
Better handling of packet loss across streams

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Protocol improvements do not remove the need to reduce unnecessary requests and waterfalls.

topics.title: Browser Caching
importance: Correct caching improves loading speed, reduces backend traffic, and creates more responsive interfaces.

groups.title: Key things
prose: Different cache layers have different ownership, lifetime, and invalidation rules.

groups.title: Critical distinction
items: no-cache: may store but must revalidate before reuse.
no-store: do not store the response.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: ETags can offer more precise validation than timestamps.

groups.title: Recommended pattern
prose: For versioned JavaScript, CSS, and images:
A content change creates a different filename.

groups.title: Important things
items: Avoid excessively long immutable caching for HTML.
Ensure users receive updated asset references after deployment.
prose: HTML often references the latest asset files.

topics.title: Storage, Cookies, and Client Persistence
importance: Storage decisions affect authentication, security, performance, offline behavior, and multi-tab coordination.

groups.title: HttpOnly
items: Reduces token theft through direct script access.
Does not prevent all XSS impact.

groups.title: Secure
items: Cookie is sent only over HTTPS.

groups.title: Important things
items: Cookies are automatically included only when request rules match.
Cross-origin requests may require credential configuration.

groups.title: Important things
items: Shared by tabs under the same origin.
Vulnerable to theft when XSS exists.
Suitable for non-sensitive preferences and cached state.

groups.title: Important things
items: Scoped to a browsing context or tab.
Usually cleared when the tab closes.
Not automatically shared like local storage.

topics.title: Same-Origin Policy and CORS
importance: This topic is essential for applications running on different domains, subdomains, ports, and backend environments.

groups.title: Important things
items: Restricts how one origin interacts with another.
Protects users from malicious websites reading sensitive data from authenticated sites.
Does not prevent all cross-origin requests from being sent.
Often prevents JavaScript from reading the response.

groups.title: Overview
prose: A request may avoid preflight when it meets strict conditions regarding:

groups.title: Important caution
prose: “Simple” does not mean automatically permitted. The server still needs appropriate CORS headers.

groups.title: Important things
items: Browser asks whether the actual request is permitted.
Server responds with allowed methods, origins, and headers.
The browser then decides whether to send the actual request.

groups.title: Important restriction
prose: Credentialed CORS requests cannot use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *.

groups.title: Overview
items: Redirect response missing required CORS headers

topics.title: HTML Fundamentals and Semantics
importance: HTML provides structure, accessibility, browser behavior, SEO signals, and native interaction.

groups.title: Key things
items: Screen readers use landmarks for navigation.
Do not choose elements only based on default appearance.

groups.title: Important things
items: Use headings to represent document hierarchy.
Do not select heading levels solely for font size.
Avoid skipping levels without a structural reason.
Each major section should have a meaningful heading.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: A clickable div does not automatically provide keyboard and accessibility behavior.

groups.title: Key things
items: Every input needs an accessible name.
Group related controls with fieldset and legend.
Display errors near fields and connect them programmatically.

groups.title: Key things
items: Support keyboard and screen-reader interaction for interactive tables.

topics.title: Accessibility
importance: Accessibility is part of software correctness. An interface that excludes keyboard or assistive-technology users is incomplete.

groups.title: Perceivable
prose: Users must be able to perceive content.

groups.title: Operable
prose: Users must be able to operate controls.

groups.title: Understandable
prose: Interfaces and content should behave predictably.

groups.title: Robust
prose: Content should work with browsers and assistive technologies.

groups.title: Important things
items: Every interactive control must be keyboard accessible.
Tab order should follow logical visual order.
Support Enter and Space according to expected control behavior.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Do not move focus unexpectedly without a clear user-action reason.

groups.title: Important rules
items: Do not add ARIA when native behavior already exists.
ARIA changes accessibility semantics, not actual behavior.
A custom button still needs keyboard handling even with role="button".

groups.title: Important things
items: Use polite announcements for normal updates.

groups.title: Important things
items: Disabled text should still be understandable.
Do not communicate status only with colour.
Test light and dark themes separately.

topics.title: CSS Fundamentals
importance: CSS mastery requires understanding layout rules, the cascade, sizing, stacking, and rendering—not just memorizing properties.

groups.title: Key things
items: The cascade determines which declaration wins.
!important should not be the default solution.
Cascade layers can help separate reset, base, component, and utility rules.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Use inherit, initial, unset, and revert intentionally.

groups.title: box-sizing
prose: This usually makes sizing more predictable.

groups.title: Important things
items: Absolutely positioned elements use a containing block.
Fixed positioning may behave differently inside transformed ancestors.
Overusing absolute positioning creates fragile layouts.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: A child cannot escape its parent stacking context merely by using a very large z-index.

topics.title: Flexbox
importance: Flexbox is the primary one-dimensional layout system for rows, columns, alignment, and distributing available space.

groups.title: Key things
items: flex-basis defines the initial main-size basis.
Shrinking behavior is influenced by intrinsic content size.
min-width: auto can prevent expected shrinking.
min-width: 0 often fixes overflowing flex children.

groups.title: Important distinction
prose: align-content matters when multiple flex lines exist.

groups.title: Important things
items: Grid may be better when row and column alignment both matter.

topics.title: CSS Grid
importance: Grid is designed for two-dimensional layouts where rows and columns need coordinated control.

groups.title: Key things
items: auto-fit and auto-fill behave differently with empty tracks.
Intrinsic sizing is important for content-aware layouts.

groups.title: Key things
items: Explicit placement can improve layout clarity.
Avoid creating visual order that conflicts with DOM and keyboard order.

groups.title: Important things
items: Allows nested layouts to align with parent grid tracks.
Useful for cards and forms requiring consistent internal alignment.
Verify browser support according to project requirements.

topics.title: Responsive Design
importance: Responsive design must account for screen size, container size, input method, content size, and user preferences.

groups.title: Important things
items: Start with essential content and behavior.
Avoid assuming mobile means lower capability.
Consider touch target size and mobile keyboard behavior.

groups.title: Key things
prose: Do not base every breakpoint on specific device models. Choose breakpoints where the layout requires change.

groups.title: Important things
items: Components respond to available container space.
Useful for reusable components in different layouts.
Reduces dependency on global viewport breakpoints.

groups.title: Key things
items: Use rem for scalable typography and spacing.
Use dynamic viewport units carefully on mobile.
clamp can create fluid values with minimum and maximum limits.

groups.title: Key things
items: Avoid serving desktop-sized images to small screens.
Provide dimensions to reduce layout shifts.
Use art direction when crops need to change across layouts.

topics.title: CSS Architecture and Design Tokens
importance: Large applications need predictable styling, theming, component ownership, and reusable design decisions.

groups.title: Key things
items: Avoid directly using raw colour values everywhere.
Dark mode should remap semantic meaning rather than individually overriding components.

groups.title: Important things
items: Use CSS custom properties where appropriate.
Support system theme and explicit user preference.
Ensure every token has a dark-theme equivalent.

topics.title: TypeScript Fundamentals
importance: TypeScript improves correctness, refactoring, API clarity, and developer tooling, but only when its type model is properly understood.

groups.title: Key things
items: TypeScript checks code during development or build.
Types are removed from emitted JavaScript.
TypeScript cannot automatically validate backend responses.
Runtime validation is still required for untrusted data.

groups.title: Important things
items: Add explicit types at important boundaries.
Inference may widen literals unless constrained.

groups.title: Important definition
prose: Types are compatible based on their structure.
This is usually allowed because the required structure exists.

groups.title: any
items: Should be limited to controlled migration boundaries.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Union types represent one of several possibilities.

groups.title: Important things
Caution
prose: Conflicting property types may create impossible types.

topics.title: Type Narrowing and Domain Modeling
importance: Narrowing allows TypeScript to safely determine which member of a union is being used.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Truthiness narrowing can accidentally treat valid values such as 0 and "" as absent.

groups.title: Example
Key things
items: Each state has a common discriminant.
Each state contains only relevant properties.

groups.title: Overview
Key benefit
prose: New domain states cause compiler errors until every required branch is handled.

groups.title: Overview
Important caution
prose: A wrong type guard misleads the compiler. It must validate accurately.

topics.title: TypeScript Generics
importance: Generics preserve relationships between inputs and outputs while supporting reusable abstractions.

groups.title: Overview
Key things
items: T represents a type chosen during usage.
Generics preserve exact information that unknown or broad unions may lose.

groups.title: Overview
Key things
prose: Constraints ensure a required minimum structure while preserving additional fields.

groups.title: Overview
Key things
items: Indexed access returns the corresponding property type.
This pattern helps build safe reusable utilities.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Do not create generic APIs so abstract that ordinary usage becomes difficult.

topics.title: Advanced TypeScript
importance: Advanced types help model libraries, domain APIs, reusable components, and strongly typed configuration.

groups.title: Overview
Overview
code: type IsString<T> = T extends string ? true : false;

groups.title: Important things
items: Can become difficult to understand when deeply nested.

groups.title: Overview
code:
type PromiseValue<T> =
  T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T;

groups.title: Key things
prose: infer extracts a type from a matched structure.

groups.title: Overview
Benefit
prose: Prevents accidentally passing one string-based identifier where another is expected.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Deep recursive types can slow the compiler and reduce readability.

topics.title: Runtime Validation and API Contracts
importance: TypeScript interfaces do not prove that server data is valid.

groups.title: Parsing
prose: Checks the actual runtime structure and returns either:

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Shared contracts should not tightly couple frontend release timing to every backend implementation detail.

topics.title: React Mental Model
importance: React should be understood as a rendering and state coordination model, not merely a collection of hooks.

groups.title: Important things
items: Components are functions or classes that describe UI.
They should remain pure during render.
Rendering may happen more than once.
Do not perform irreversible side effects during render.

groups.title: Important things
items: React elements are descriptions, not DOM nodes.
They contain type, props, and child information.
React compares descriptions to determine changes.

groups.title: Critical rule
prose: The render phase should be pure and repeatable.

groups.title: Important things
items: Applies changes that users can observe.

groups.title: Important things
prose: React compares the previous and next element trees.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Changing element type or key can reset state.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Keys identify list items across renders.

topics.title: React State
importance: Correct state design prevents synchronization bugs, unnecessary rendering, and unclear ownership.

groups.title: Important questions
items: Does it need to survive navigation?
Is the server the true owner?
Should it exist in the URL?

groups.title: Key principle
prose: Keep state as close as possible to the components that need it.

groups.title: Overview
prose: Do not store values that can be derived.

groups.title: Important things
items: State does not change immediately within the current render.
Use functional updates when the next value depends on the previous one.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Do not mutate React state directly.

groups.title: Uncontrolled
prose: State is managed by the DOM or component internally.

groups.title: Key things
items: Uncontrolled inputs may reduce rendering overhead.
Component libraries may support both patterns.
Avoid switching between controlled and uncontrolled modes.

groups.title: Useful when
items: Error and retry behavior is complex.

topics.title: React Hooks
importance: Hooks connect component rendering with state, references, context, memoization, and external systems.

groups.title: Rules
items: Call hooks only at the top level.
Call hooks from React components or custom hooks.

groups.title: Why
prose: React associates hook state with call order.

groups.title: Important things
items: Initializer functions can avoid repeated expensive initialization.
State should represent meaningful domain or UI information.

groups.title: Key things
items: Avoid actions such as SET_EVERYTHING without a clear domain reason.

groups.title: Key things
items: Updating a ref does not trigger rendering.
Do not use refs as hidden replacement state when UI should update.
Refs are useful for imperative interactions.

groups.title: Purpose
prose: Caches a calculated value between renders.

groups.title: Appropriate when
items: Stable reference helps memoized children or hooks.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Memoization has overhead and does not guarantee permanent caching.

groups.title: Appropriate when
items: Stable callbacks are required by subscriptions or dependencies.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Do not wrap every handler automatically.

groups.title: Good custom hooks
items: Hide complex subscriptions or API coordination.
Expose a focused and stable interface.

topics.title: React Effects
importance: Effects are one of the most misunderstood parts of React. They should synchronize with external systems, not replace normal rendering logic.

groups.title: Overview
items: Logging connected to committed UI state

groups.title: Overview
items: Updating state from other state without necessity
Handling user events that can be handled directly
Resetting data that can be controlled by keys or state design

groups.title: Important things
items: Bugs that appear only after later renders
prose: Dependencies should include values used from the component scope.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Do not silence dependency warnings without understanding the actual data flow.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Cleanup runs before the effect reruns and when the component unmounts.

groups.title: Common problem
prose: Old asynchronous work finishes after new work and updates the component incorrectly.

groups.title: Important things
items: Run effect setup and cleanup again.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Do not “fix” the duplicate behavior by disabling Strict Mode. Fix the effect design.

topics.title: React Context
importance: Context distributes values through a component tree without manually passing props through every layer.

groups.title: Important things
prose: When the provider value changes, consumers may rerender.

groups.title: Optimization approaches
items: Keep high-frequency state outside broad context providers.

groups.title: Important things
items: Safe default or explicit provider requirement

groups.title: Important caution
prose: A single application context containing every state value becomes difficult to maintain and optimize.

topics.title: React Forms
importance: Forms contain complicated combinations of state, validation, accessibility, server errors, dynamic fields, and submission behavior.

groups.title: Key things
items: Do not validate on every keystroke when it harms usability.
Preserve backend error details in a field-friendly format.

groups.title: Important things
items: Focus the first invalid field when appropriate.
Handle network and business errors separately.
Support cancellation for long-running actions where possible.

groups.title: Important things
items: Preserve values during controlled visibility changes when required.
Avoid using array indexes as permanent field identity.
Separate form schema from rendered field components.

topics.title: React Error Handling
importance: Failure isolation prevents a small UI problem from breaking the entire application.

groups.title: Do not automatically handle
items: Server request errors unless they affect rendering

groups.title: Key principle
prose: Place boundaries where meaningful recovery is possible.

groups.title: Overview
prose: Should provide relevant actions such as:

topics.title: React Concurrency and Suspense Concepts
importance: Modern React can prioritize work and coordinate loading boundaries to keep interfaces responsive.

groups.title: Key things
prose: Transitions mark updates that may be deferred.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Transitions do not make expensive code free. They improve scheduling and responsiveness.

groups.title: Important things
items: Debouncing is not the exact desired behavior.
prose: A deferred value allows slower UI to lag behind urgent input.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Suspense coordinates waiting UI for supported asynchronous resources.

topics.title: Frontend State Classification
importance: The correct state solution depends on what kind of state is being managed.

topics.title: Redux and Redux Toolkit
importance: Redux is useful for predictable shared state, complex domain transitions, debugging, middleware, and coordinated updates.

groups.title: Important things
items: State should be serializable where possible.
Avoid placing transient component details globally.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Actions should preferably describe domain events rather than only property assignment.

groups.title: Important things
items: Should not read current time randomly.
Redux Toolkit allows mutation-like syntax through Immer.

groups.title: Example
Important caution
prose: Middleware should not become an invisible place containing all application business logic.

topics.title: Server-State Management
importance: Server state requires caching, freshness rules, deduplication, invalidation, mutation coordination, and background synchronization.

groups.title: Important things
prose: A query key should include every input that changes the result.

groups.title: Important things
items: How long unused cache is retained

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Multiple components requesting the same resource should ideally share the same in-flight operation and cached result.

topics.title: API Client Architecture
importance: A centralized API architecture prevents inconsistent request behavior, duplicated error handling, and tight coupling between components and backend transport.

groups.title: Overview
code:
authApi
usersApi
casesApi
workflowApi
layoutApi
searchApi

groups.title: Key things
items: Avoid exposing raw endpoint details everywhere.
Keep transport details inside the API layer.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Different categories need different recovery and messages.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Interceptors can hide flow and create loops. Authentication retries must be tightly controlled.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Map backend shapes into frontend models at boundaries.

topics.title: Authentication
importance: Authentication architecture affects security, routing, deep links, multi-tab behavior, refresh behavior, and every API request.

groups.title: Authorization
prose: Determines what the user may do.

groups.title: Critical rule
prose: Frontend checks are for user experience. Backend enforcement is mandatory.

groups.title: High-level flow
items: User is redirected to identity provider.
Identity provider redirects back with authorization code.
Tokens or backend session are established.

groups.title: Important things
items: Redirect URI must match configured values.

groups.title: Characteristics
items: Refresh and multi-tab behavior require careful design.

groups.title: Benefits
items: Tokens are not directly available to frontend JavaScript.
Frontend auth logic may become simpler.

groups.title: Correct startup questions
items: Which authentication mode should be used?
Is there already a valid application session?
Is the current route an authentication callback?
Should the original deep link be preserved?
Should the app redirect or continue?
Has a redirect already been attempted?

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Authentication initialization should be modeled as an explicit state machine.

groups.title: Avoid
prose: A boolean cannot represent the full lifecycle.

groups.title: Required behavior
items: Application stores the complete intended destination.
User returns to the original destination.

groups.title: Important things
items: Preserve path, query, and fragment when appropriate.
Handle deep links opened in a new tab.

groups.title: Important considerations
items: Another tab should detect the new session.
Session expiry should not trigger repeated redirects in every tab.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: These are different failures and may require different handling.

groups.title: Possible steps
items: Optionally sign out from identity provider.

topics.title: Authorization and Permissions
importance: Frontend authorization architecture should remain consistent, testable, and aligned with backend policy.

groups.title: Limitation
prose: Role checks become rigid when permissions vary by resource or operation.

groups.title: Overview
items: User can edit cases they own.
User can approve only within their region.
User can view fields based on classification.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: The frontend may need resource metadata, but the backend must enforce the rule.

groups.title: Important thing
prose: Avoid briefly rendering restricted UI before permissions are resolved.

topics.title: Real-Time Communication
importance: Applications may need background updates, workflow progress, notifications, collaboration, or live dashboards.

groups.title: Important things
items: Pause or reduce when the tab is hidden.

groups.title: Important things
items: Server holds request until data is available or timeout occurs.
More efficient than frequent empty polling.
Still built on repeated HTTP requests.

groups.title: Characteristics
items: Useful for notifications and progress streams

groups.title: Limitations
items: Authentication and proxy behavior need planning
Event replay requires IDs and server support

groups.title: Characteristics
items: Suitable for collaboration, chat, and highly interactive updates

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Never assume every network event arrives exactly once and in perfect order.

topics.title: Performance Engineering
importance: Performance is not only loading speed. It includes interaction latency, rendering cost, memory, responsiveness, and stability.

groups.title: Key principle
prose: Measure before and after every meaningful optimization.

groups.title: Key things
items: Different devices and networks produce different results.
An average can hide poor experiences for slower users.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Budgets should be enforced in CI or monitoring, not only written in documentation.

topics.title: React Performance
importance: React optimization should target real expensive work, not simply the number of renders.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Rendering is normal. Expensive unnecessary rendering is the problem.

groups.title: Important things
items: Keep frequently changing state close to its usage.
Separate expensive static regions from dynamic regions.
Avoid lifting state higher than required.
Avoid huge components that recalculate everything.

groups.title: Problems
items: New object and function references every render.

groups.title: Important accessibility consideration
prose: Virtualized lists need careful keyboard navigation, screen-reader behavior, and scroll restoration.

topics.title: Bundle Performance
importance: JavaScript must be downloaded, parsed, compiled, and executed. Large bundles affect all four stages.

groups.title: Important things
items: Works best with static ES module syntax.
Importing from package root may include more code than expected.

groups.title: Key things
items: Do not create excessive tiny chunks.

groups.title: Important questions
items: Is it loaded on the initial route?
Can a native browser API replace it?

groups.title: Important things
items: May expose source structure if publicly accessible.
Upload privately to monitoring platforms where possible.
Ensure release versions match source maps.

topics.title: Memory Management
importance: Memory leaks cause slowdowns, crashes, and degraded long-running application sessions.

groups.title: Key things
items: Developers do not manually free normal JavaScript memory.

groups.title: Important thing
prose: A removed DOM node can remain in memory if JavaScript still references it.

groups.title: Investigation
items: Repeat the problematic workflow and compare snapshots.

groups.title: Key things
prose: An unbounded cache is a controlled memory leak.

topics.title: Frontend Testing
importance: Testing should provide confidence in user behavior, domain logic, integrations, and critical workflows.

groups.title: Key principle
prose: Test what the user sees and does, not internal implementation details.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Mock network boundaries rather than mocking every internal function.

groups.title: Key things
items: Collect screenshots, videos, and traces for failures.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Contract tests verify frontend assumptions about backend behavior.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Automated tools catch only a portion of accessibility problems.

topics.title: Frontend Security
importance: Frontend code executes in an untrusted user-controlled environment. Security requires browser, backend, and infrastructure cooperation.

groups.title: Important things
items: Sanitization must match the output context.
Never trust stored data merely because it came from your database.

groups.title: Important things
prose: CSRF is relevant when the browser automatically attaches credentials such as cookies.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: CORS is not a complete CSRF defense.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Protect sensitive applications from being invisibly embedded in malicious pages.

groups.title: Problem
prose: An attacker supplies a malicious return URL.

groups.title: Defense
items: Or validate against an explicit allowlist.
Never redirect to arbitrary user-provided URLs.

groups.title: Critical rule
prose: Anything included in a frontend bundle must be considered public.

groups.title: Important practices
items: Avoid adding packages for trivial functions.
Review third-party scripts separately from package dependencies.

topics.title: Reliability and Failure Handling
importance: Production applications must continue behaving usefully when networks, APIs, sessions, or individual features fail.

groups.title: Important things
items: Every request should have an acceptable waiting limit.
Timeout values depend on operation type.
Long-running jobs may use async processing instead of holding requests.
Timeout UI should provide recovery options.

groups.title: Important things
items: Retry only safe and temporary failures.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Do not replace the entire dashboard with one error if partial content remains useful.

groups.title: Overview
items: Show cached data when live data fails.

groups.title: Important considerations
items: Requests can fail even while the browser reports online.

groups.title: Defenses
items: Avoid assuming a timeout means the server did nothing.

topics.title: Frontend Architecture
importance: Architecture defines boundaries, ownership, dependency direction, change isolation, and how teams work safely at scale.

groups.title: Example structure
code:
features/
  authentication/
  entity-management/
  layout-builder/
  form-renderer/
  entity-search/
  workflow/

groups.title: Key principle
items: Domain logic should not import React.
Components should not know raw endpoint URLs.
Feature code should not import another feature’s private files.
prose: Higher-level business logic should not depend directly on low-level implementation details.

groups.title: Good pattern
Key thing
prose: Public APIs preserve encapsulation and allow internal refactoring.

groups.title: Avoid
prose: A generic shared folder containing unrelated code from every feature.

topics.title: Component Architecture
importance: Good component boundaries improve reuse, accessibility, performance, testing, and maintenance.

groups.title: Responsibilities
items: Convert domain state into presentation props.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Do not turn every component into a container. Use the boundary only where it improves clarity.

groups.title: Key questions
items: Does the API express user intent?
Are controlled and uncontrolled modes clear?

topics.title: Design Systems
importance: A design system combines visual language, reusable components, accessibility, governance, and engineering standards.

topics.title: Monorepo Architecture
importance: A monorepo can improve code sharing and coordinated tooling, but only when dependency boundaries are explicit.

groups.title: Important things
items: Apps may depend on feature and shared libraries.
Features may depend on domain and infrastructure interfaces.
Domain libraries may not depend on React.
Shared UI may not depend on product features.
One application should not import another application.

groups.title: Important things
items: Include source, dependencies, configuration, and environment assumptions.
Separate deterministic tasks from environment-dependent tasks.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Allow controlled application overrides. One configuration should not force unrelated apps into identical architecture.

topics.title: Microfrontends
importance: Microfrontends are an organizational and deployment architecture, not merely a code-splitting technique.

groups.title: Overview
items: Tight runtime knowledge of internal implementation

groups.title: Important questions
items: What happens if one microfrontend fails to load?
How are errors attributed to owning teams?

topics.title: Backend for Frontend
importance: A BFF provides APIs tailored to frontend workflows and can securely manage sessions and downstream orchestration.

subtopics.title: What Should Not Be Added Carelessly

groups.title: Overview
items: A dumping ground for all business rules
A tightly coupled frontend release blocker

topics.title: Feature Flags and Remote Configuration
importance: Feature flags separate deployment from release and allow controlled rollout, experimentation, and emergency shutdown.

groups.title: Important distinction
prose: A feature flag is not automatically an authorization mechanism.

groups.title: Important questions
items: What happens when the service fails?
How do we prevent UI flicker?

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Permanent old flags create hidden branches and long-term complexity.

topics.title: Observability
importance: Observability helps reconstruct failures, measure user impact, and connect frontend behavior with backend systems.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Never log sensitive authentication or business data.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Group errors carefully so distinct root causes are not hidden together.

groups.title: Required controls
items: Consent and legal review where necessary

groups.title: Flow
items: Frontend generates or receives an ID.
Logs and traces include the same ID.
Support can reconstruct the complete operation.

topics.title: CI/CD and Frontend Delivery
importance: A reliable delivery pipeline validates quality, produces immutable artifacts, and supports safe release and rollback.

groups.title: Key things
prose: Runtime configuration can allow one immutable artifact to run across environments, but initialization and security must be designed carefully.

groups.title: Important things
items: Do not silently modify files after verification.
Associate source maps and monitoring releases correctly.

topics.title: Frontend System Design
importance: System design connects product requirements with architecture, data flow, performance, security, reliability, and team ownership.

topics.title: Architecture Documentation
importance: Architecture that exists only in one engineer’s mind cannot be safely maintained.

topics.title: AI-Powered Frontend Applications
importance: AI applications introduce probabilistic output, streaming, tool execution, safety, structured data validation, and cost concerns.

groups.title: Important things
items: Prevent markdown or HTML rendering vulnerabilities.

groups.title: Important things
items: Clearly separate facts from generated suggestions.
Avoid pretending uncertain output is confirmed.

groups.title: Key thing
prose: Do not evaluate only by reading a few manually selected responses. Build repeatable evaluation datasets.

topics.title: Git and Engineering Collaboration
importance: Senior frontend engineers must understand version control beyond basic commit and push operations.

groups.title: Rebase
items: Replays commits on a new base.

groups.title: Important caution
prose: Avoid rebasing shared public history without team agreement.

groups.title: Use cases
items: Apply a specific fix to another branch.

groups.title: Important things
prose: git bisect uses binary search to identify the commit that introduced a regression.

groups.title: Strong use case
prose: Combine it with automated tests to locate a failure efficiently.

topics.title: Internationalization and Localization
importance: Global applications must support different languages, currencies, dates, numbers, directions, and time zones.

groups.title: Important things
items: Support gender and grammatical variation where needed.
Avoid placing markup directly inside translation logic without structure.

groups.title: Important things
items: Store timestamps in a consistent absolute format.
Display using the user’s locale and time zone.

groups.title: Important things
items: Do not mirror universally recognizable icons incorrectly.
Test forms, tables, navigation, and charts.

groups.title: Important things
prose: Translated text may be much longer than English.

topics.title: Progressive Web Applications and Offline Systems
importance: PWA and offline support require deliberate caching, synchronization, updates, and conflict management.

groups.title: Important things
items: Have an installation and activation lifecycle.

groups.title: Stale while revalidate
items: Data where quick display and background refresh are acceptable

groups.title: Important things
items: A new Service Worker may wait while old pages remain open.
Users can become stuck on old assets if updates are poorly managed.
Update prompts should avoid interrupting critical user work.
Cache versions must be cleaned up.

groups.title: Overview
prose: For every major topic, you should be able to answer all of the following.

groups.title: Overview
items: What problem does this concept solve?
Which browser, JavaScript, or React mechanisms are involved?

groups.title: Overview
items: Can you implement a simplified version?
Can you write tests for it?
Can you handle loading, success, failure, and cleanup?

groups.title: Overview
items: What are the most common bugs?
Which developer tools should be used?
How would you reproduce the problem?
How would you confirm the root cause?

groups.title: Overview
items: When should this concept be used?
When should it not be used?
How does it behave at enterprise scale?

groups.title: Overview
items: How should failure and rollback work?

groups.title: Overview
items: Can you explain it without jargon?
Can you explain it to another senior engineer?
Can you defend your design decision during an architecture review?
